metric_name: Expert witness career initiation
metric_value: Received unsolicited call from unknown law firm seeking expert on enterprise database technology; said yes, opening a parallel career spanning 20+ years.
source_page: Opening paragraphs

metric_name: First case type
metric_value: Complex commercial litigation involving enterprise database technology; required expertise in relational databases at architectural and market level.
source_page: Opening paragraphs

metric_name: Parallel career duration
metric_value: Expert witness practice ran alongside Aberdeen research practice for more than two decades (1992–2015).
source_page: Opening paragraphs

metric_name: Consulting vs. testifying expert distinction
metric_value: Consulting expert: confidential, candid, not disclosed. Testifying expert: formal opinions, depositions, cross-examination — every opinion must be bulletproof.
source_page: What an Expert Witness Actually Does

metric_name: Routes into technology expert witness work in 1990s
metric_value: Three routes: former senior engineers, former CIOs, and technology analysts with research credentials. Analyst credential proved advantageous.
source_page: What an Expert Witness Actually Does

metric_name: Aberdeen credentials accepted by courts
metric_value: Aberdeen’s published research and TPC work gave Kastner credentials courts found credible for technology expert testimony.
source_page: What an Expert Witness Actually Does

metric_name: Analyst advantage over engineer in litigation
metric_value: As analyst (not engineer), Kastner could speak to what industry knew, what market expected, and what competent technology practice looked like — the standards by which decisions are judged.
source_page: What an Expert Witness Actually Does

metric_name: Case subject matter range
metric_value: Cases ranged from database licensing disputes to software project failures to antitrust claims in the storage market.
source_page: What an Expert Witness Actually Does

metric_name: Safeway litigation characterization
metric_value: Most significant litigation engagement of Kastner’s expert witness career: major antitrust and breach-of-contract dispute.
source_page: The Safeway Data Warehouse Case

metric_name: Safeway dispute core issues
metric_value: Centered on data warehousing technology: what had been promised, what had been delivered, whether competitive market analysis informing the deal was sound, and damages.
source_page: The Safeway Data Warehouse Case

metric_name: Kastner role in Safeway case
metric_value: Second-seat to novice expert Hugh Bishop in the Safeway engagement.
source_page: The Safeway Data Warehouse Case

metric_name: Data warehouses as strategic retail infrastructure
metric_value: By mid-1990s, data warehouses had become strategic infrastructure for large retailers, consolidating POS, supplier, and inventory data for analytical decision-making.
source_page: Sidebar: Data Warehousing as Legal Battleground

metric_name: Safeway competitive implications of data warehouse
metric_value: Better analytical capability translated directly into better purchasing negotiations, more effective promotions, and reduced inventory costs — all affecting margins in a thin-margin business.
source_page: Sidebar: Data Warehousing as Legal Battleground

metric_name: Safeway Teradata machine destruction
metric_value: Safeway’s Teradata data warehouse machine was effectively destroyed when the fire extinguishing system exploded into the closed computer room.
source_page: The Safeway Data Warehouse Case

metric_name: Kastner damages expert role in Safeway
metric_value: Role was damages expert: built analytical model to answer what market value of contracted technology was, how it differed from delivery, and economic consequence of that gap.
source_page: The Safeway Data Warehouse Case

metric_name: Aberdeen frameworks applied in Safeway litigation
metric_value: Drew on Aberdeen’s competitive landscape analysis, TCO models, and performance benchmarking methodology; research and litigation used the same analytical tools in different contexts.
source_page: The Safeway Data Warehouse Case

metric_name: Safeway engagement duration
metric_value: Ran over three years, involved dozens of depositions, produced hundreds of pages of expert reports.
source_page: The Safeway Data Warehouse Case

metric_name: Safeway case outcome
metric_value: Settlement terms confidential; technical and economic analysis admitted into evidence; established market value of enterprise data warehousing in terms courts could understand.
source_page: The Safeway Data Warehouse Case

metric_name: Expert testimony contribution in technology disputes
metric_value: Case established market value of enterprise data warehousing capabilities in terms courts could understand and apply — described as the fundamental contribution of expert testimony.
source_page: The Safeway Data Warehouse Case

metric_name: Data warehouse damages quantifiability
metric_value: When a data warehouse project failed or underperformed, damages were calculable but only with expert analysis quantifying properly functioning vs. failed system performance.
source_page: Sidebar: Data Warehousing as Legal Battleground

metric_name: Software project failure pattern — scope never locked
metric_value: Initial contracts described systems at a level of abstraction both parties could agree to but left implementation details undefined; client and vendor diverged on what the system should do.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Software project failure pattern — requirements changed faster than contracts
metric_value: Business environments change; ERP implementations starting 1999 encountered Y2K remediation priorities in 1998; change orders introduced adversarial dynamics.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Software project failure pattern — integration underspecified
metric_value: New system always had to connect to existing systems never adequately described in the contract; integration scope was the site of the most damaging disputes.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Software project failure pattern — testing inadequate
metric_value: User acceptance testing frequently rushed to meet contractual go-live dates; defects discovered in production were far more expensive to remediate.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Standish CHAOS Report IT project success rates
metric_value: Roughly 30% succeed as defined; 50% succeed partially; 20% fail completely. Rates have improved modestly with agile, cloud, SaaS but persist over 30 years.
source_page: Sidebar: Why IT Projects Fail at Such Consistent Rates

metric_name: Root cause of IT project failures
metric_value: Technical failure was real but not root cause; root cause was governance failure: inadequate requirements, insufficient change management, misaligned incentives, or executive override.
source_page: Sidebar: Why IT Projects Fail at Such Consistent Rates

metric_name: Genuine technical incompetence vs. governance failure frequency
metric_value: In 20 years of expert witness work on software project failures, encountered genuine technical incompetence perhaps 5 times; encountered governance and organizational failures in every single case.
source_page: Sidebar: Why IT Projects Fail at Such Consistent Rates

metric_name: Expert report questions in software project failures
metric_value: Three questions: (1) What did the contract actually require? (2) What was actually delivered? (3) What were the consequences?
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Contract interpretation standard in technology disputes
metric_value: Contracts frequently ambiguous; question is what an objective, reasonable reader would understand given industry standards and practices.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Damages categories in failed IT projects
metric_value: Include: cost of work paid for but not received; cost of remediation; operational losses during underperformance period; competitive disadvantage from market position loss.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Aberdeen market research value in damages analysis
metric_value: Quantifying competitive consequences of technology failure required understanding what properly functioning technology would have enabled — exactly the comparative analysis Aberdeen performed for advisory clients.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: ADL forensic methodology transfer to expert witness work
metric_value: Forensic habits from ADL — detailed records, spotting scope creep and governance failures, reading a project log like a doctor reads a chart — proved directly applicable.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Florida welfare bid overview
metric_value: EDS and IBM bid on $200M statewide Florida human services application; RFP required specific terminal response times on average.
source_page: The Florida Welfare Bid

metric_name: Unisys retains Kastner for Florida bid protest
metric_value: Unisys, a losing bidder, contacted Kastner to serve as expert witness to prove IBM’s three-tier mainframe system could not meet RFP specifications.
source_page: The Florida Welfare Bid

metric_name: IBM performance documentation in Florida case
metric_value: IBM delivered a box of computer paper with 525 performance simulation runs and nothing else.
source_page: The Florida Welfare Bid

metric_name: Kastner finds needle in haystack in IBM performance data
metric_value: Found critical data point in 525-run IBM simulation; testified IBM’s own performance estimator showed the system would have 20–30 second average response times.
source_page: The Florida Welfare Bid

metric_name: IBM Florida welfare system RFP response time issue
metric_value: IBM’s own performance estimator indicated 20–30 second average response times on a system whose RFP required specific lower thresholds.
source_page: The Florida Welfare Bid

metric_name: Florida bid protest outcome
metric_value: The protest lost; Kastner returned to his day-to-day work.
source_page: The Florida Welfare Bid

metric_name: Florida welfare system actual cost and failure
metric_value: System was built at $200M cost; failed acceptance tests with 20–30 second response times — exactly as Kastner had predicted.
source_page: The Florida Welfare Bid

metric_name: Florida welfare bid corruption
metric_value: Several state officials were indicted over the bid after the system’s failure.
source_page: The Florida Welfare Bid

metric_name: Congressional response to Florida welfare system failure
metric_value: Congress held hearings and passed two laws preventing unfettered contracts like Florida’s; Congress had put up most of the lost millions.
source_page: The Florida Welfare Bid

metric_name: Kastner vindication in Florida case
metric_value: Kastner notes he was vindicated when the system failed exactly as his analysis predicted, despite losing the original bid protest.
source_page: The Florida Welfare Bid

metric_name: Florida case documentation gap
metric_value: Kastner notes the Florida account is incomplete; needs to flesh out with more factual data from state archives while preparing memoirs.
source_page: The Florida Welfare Bid

metric_name: Expert witness vantage point on technology markets
metric_value: Courts see markets through transactions that failed; most market participants experience markets through transactions that succeeded. Expert witness work provides the failure-side perspective.
source_page: What the Courtroom Taught About Technology Markets

metric_name: Contracts describe aspirations not obligations
metric_value: Enterprise technology contracts are frequently written in language both parties understand differently; ambiguity is sometimes how deals get done; cost paid at implementation or in court.
source_page: What the Courtroom Taught About Technology Markets

metric_name: Technical credibility as scarcest resource in technology disputes
metric_value: Attorneys understand legal concepts deeply and technology superficially; tech experts vice versa; value of an expert understanding both dimensions is disproportionate to their fee.
source_page: What the Courtroom Taught About Technology Markets

metric_name: Organizational failure precedes technical failure
metric_value: In software project disputes, the technical problems that ended up in court were almost never the first sign of trouble; before code was bad, governance was bad; before system failed, communication had failed.
source_page: What the Courtroom Taught About Technology Markets

metric_name: Market research and litigation analysis use the same tools
metric_value: Aberdeen’s analytical frameworks — market definition, competitive comparison, TCO modeling, performance benchmarking — were the same frameworks courts needed. Translation was only methodological.
source_page: What the Courtroom Taught About Technology Markets

metric_name: Research vs. litigation presentation of analysis
metric_value: Research conclusions presented with confidence levels; legal opinions presented with certainty. The underlying analysis is identical.
source_page: What the Courtroom Taught About Technology Markets

metric_name: Expert witness practice end date
metric_value: Active expert witness practice wound down around 2015, concurrent with transition away from independent consulting work following Aberdeen departure.
source_page: The End of the Practice

metric_name: Lasting rigor instilled by expert witness work
metric_value: Every analysis since 2015 carries the discipline: defend every number, document every assumption, anticipate every objection.
source_page: The End of the Practice

metric_name: Legal system limitations for technology disputes
metric_value: Judges and juries asked to make technical judgments they are rarely equipped to make; adversarial process poorly designed for understanding complex technical systems where truth is not binary.
source_page: The End of the Practice

metric_name: Expert witness system value
metric_value: Mediates limitations of adversarial process — imperfectly, but better than the alternative of technical decisions made without any independent expertise at all.
source_page: The End of the Practice

metric_name: Core insight from expert witness work
metric_value: The gap between what technology is supposed to do and what it actually does is where most interesting problems live. In research it is called an opportunity; in litigation, a dispute. The analysis is the same.
source_page: The End of the Practice

metric_name: EDS role in Florida welfare bid
metric_value: EDS and IBM both bid on the $200M Florida statewide human services application RFP; EDS was a losing bidder alongside Unisys.
source_page: The Florida Welfare Bid
notes: Text says 'EDS and IBM bid'; Unisys was a separate losing bidder who contacted Kastner.

metric_name: Y2K remediation disrupting ERP projects
metric_value: An ERP implementation starting in 1999 encountered Y2K remediation priority in late 1998; business environment changes faster than contracts can accommodate.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Healthcare regulatory environment change example
metric_value: A healthcare system under development in 2001 faced a different regulatory environment by 2003 — illustrating how requirements change faster than contracts can accommodate.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Change order adversarial dynamics
metric_value: Change order negotiations consumed enormous energy and introduced adversarial dynamics into what should have been a collaborative client-vendor relationship.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Mental model divergence in IT contracts
metric_value: Client and vendor operated from different mental models of the same contract document; neither was necessarily acting in bad faith.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Integration scope as most damaging dispute site
metric_value: Data formats, transaction volumes, error handling requirements, and performance expectations of integrations with existing systems were discovered during implementation, not defined upfront.
source_page: The Anatomy of a Software Project Failure

metric_name: Executive pressure overriding technical judgment
metric_value: Governance failures included executive pressure that overrode technical judgment at critical decision points — a root cause found in software project failures.
source_page: Sidebar: Why IT Projects Fail at Such Consistent Rates

metric_name: TPC credentials as basis for court acceptance
metric_value: Kastner’s TPC (Transaction Processing Performance Council) work gave him performance benchmarking credentials that courts found credible.
source_page: What an Expert Witness Actually Does

metric_name: Performance benchmarking as core expert methodology
metric_value: Aberdeen’s performance benchmarking methodology was a core tool applied in litigation to evaluate whether technology performed to contracted specifications.
source_page: What an Expert Witness Actually Does

metric_name: Storage market antitrust cases
metric_value: Kastner’s expert witness work included antitrust claims in the storage market as one of the case subject matter categories.
source_page: What an Expert Witness Actually Does

metric_name: Database licensing dispute cases
metric_value: Database licensing disputes were among the categories of cases handled during Kastner’s 20+ year expert witness practice.
source_page: What an Expert Witness Actually Does
